coredata.dc:title: A novel smartphone-based nanozyme-enhanced electrochemical immunosensor for ultrasensitive direct detection of Staphylococcus aureus in milk and blood serum

Altintas, Zeynep
coredata.dc:description: A graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based nanozyme-enhanced electrochemical sensor was developed for the ultra-sensitive and rapid Staphylococcus aureus detection in PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum. The S. aureus concentration for immunosensor calibration was electrochemically determined to be 1.24× 109 CFU mL−1 (R2 = 0.99), utilizing an innovative smartphone-based electrochemical device. Transmission electron (TEM) and atomic force (AFM) microscopy analyses of GQDs revealed uniform nanoparticles (8–10 nm) with surface heights of 2 nm and 21 nm at different scan areas. Fluorescence and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectra showed an emission at 530 nm with an excitation wavelength of 485 nm, along with the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aromatic groups on the GQD surfaces. The maximum current peak for GQDs was obtained by optimizing electrochemical properties through amperometry at +0.9 V. The optimal signal intensity- and limit of detection (LOD)-based GQD concentrations were 0.0125, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1 for PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum, respectively. The optimal antibody concentration for S. aureus detection was 25 μg mL−1, resulting in a 91 % amperometric suppression and a 94 % fluorescence quenching. The developed biosensor effectively detected S. aureus, with calculated LODs of 1, 4, and 344 CFU mL−1 in PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum, respectively. TEM, AFM, and contact angle analyses confirmed S. aureus binding to the GQD-antibody bioconjugate, shifting surface height to 35 nm and contact angle from ~30° to ~65°. The immunosensor showed high specificity for S. aureus with minimal cross-reactivity to Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Enterococcus faecium, and Streptococcus pneumoniae.

GQDs characterization: TEM image (A), AFM images on the SPGE at 3 × 3 μm (B) and 1 × 1 μm (C) scales, UV–Vis and fluorescent spectra (D), FTIR spectrum (E), and CV-based electrochemical measurement (F).

(A) Functional block diagram of the developed potentiostat hardware. (B) Developed potentiostat device: circuit board (top layer), circuit board (bottom layer), assembled in an aluminum enclosure, and connected to a mobile device for operation. (C) Screens of the developed mobile application: splash screen. (D) The flowchart of electrochemical measurement using the mobile application with the developed equipment.

Amperometric signal–concentration relationships at various GQD levels for GQD-coated SPGEs in PBS buffer (A), undiluted milk (B), and blood serum (C) and their comparative analysis (D). The optimal concentration assessment of S. aureus monoclonal antibody with chrono-amperometric signals after binding with 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 antigen (E), fluorescence spectroscopy signals after binding with 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 antigen (F), and comparison of the signal-concentration relationship and fluorescence intensity for S. aureus antibody/GQDs/SPGE immobilized with 10, 25, 40 and 50 μg mL−1 antibody, following binding with 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 antigen (G).

Real-time amperometric measurements of S. aureus at different concentrations in (A) PBS, (B) undiluted milk, and (C) blood serum, as well as (D) corresponding overall amperometric responses as a function of S. aureus binding.

AFM analysis of S. aureus (1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1) binding to the sensor surface with different scan areas (A,B). Contact angle measurements of (C) GQDs, (D) GQD-antibody, and (E) GQD-antibody-antigen bioconjugation. TEM images of (F) GQDs and (G) GQD-antibody-S. aureus bioconjugation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based nanozyme-enhanced electrochemical sensor was developed for the ultra-sensitive and rapid Staphylococcus aureus detection in PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum. The S. aureus concentration for immunosensor calibration was electrochemically determined to be 1.24× 109 CFU mL−1 (R2 = 0.99), utilizing an innovative smartphone-based electrochemical device. Transmission electron (TEM) and atomic force (AFM) microscopy analyses of GQDs revealed uniform nanoparticles (8–10 nm) with surface heights of 2 nm and 21 nm at different scan areas. Fluorescence and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectra showed an emission at 530 nm with an excitation wavelength of 485 nm, along with the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aromatic groups on the GQD surfaces. The maximum current peak for GQDs was obtained by optimizing electrochemical properties through amperometry at +0.9 V. The optimal signal intensity- and limit of detection (LOD)-based GQD concentrations were 0.0125, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1 for PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum, respectively. The optimal antibody concentration for S. aureus detection was 25 μg mL−1, resulting in a 91 % amperometric suppression and a 94 % fluorescence quenching. The developed biosensor effectively detected S. aureus, with calculated LODs of 1, 4, and 344 CFU mL−1 in PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum, respectively. TEM, AFM, and contact angle analyses confirmed S. aureus binding to the GQD-antibody bioconjugate, shifting surface height to 35 nm and contact angle from ~30° to ~65°. The immunosensor showed high specificity for S. aureus with minimal cross-reactivity to Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Enterococcus faecium, and Streptococcus pneumoniae.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcus aureus is the second most common pathogen worldwide. It is a major food safety concern, causing a range of foodborne illnesses and presenting significant risks to public health globally [44]. This Gram-positive bacterium can be isolated from foods such as eggs, milk, cheese, and other dairy products. It can survive under normal conditions, at low temperatures, and even in environments with low levels of toxic substances. Owing to its potent toxicity, even minimal infective doses are capable of inducing severe health outcomes in humans, such as diarrhea, acute gastroenteritis, sepsis, acute poisoning, and even mortality in extreme cases [35,52]. Among the estimated 13.7 million global infection-related deaths in 2019, S. aureus emerged as the deadliest pathogen for individuals aged 15 years and older, accounting for 940,000 deaths [47].

For a food product to be considered safe from S. aureus infection, its concentration must remain below 1–10 CFU mL−1. However, concentrations as high as 104 CFU mL−1 are regarded as highly dangerous to human health [28]. S. aureus, known to cause mastitis, a condition that severely impacts milk yield and quality in animals [45], is associated with annual medical costs of $1 billion for humans and animals in the United States. Furthermore, S. aureus can easily transmit between humans and animals [19]. In light of its ease of transmission and substantial health threats, the early, rapid, and accurate detection of S. aureus in complex matrices such as blood serum and milk is critical for preventing foodborne illnesses, enabling early diagnosis, and ensuring food safety.

The commonly used methods for S. aureus detection, such as culture-based techniques, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and polymerase chain reaction (PCR), provide high sensitivity but are time-consuming, labor-intensive, and dependent on costly equipment [15,17,19]. Moreover, detecting S. aureus in real samples requires extensive pre-treatment procedures, such as DNA isolation and culture preparation, making these methods less practical in laboratory settings and limiting their feasibility for on-site or rapid use [56]. In contrast, a range of biosensors has been developed for S. aureus detection, integrating nanoparticles (e.g., gold, silver, platinum nanoparticles, and quantum dots) combined with recognition elements such as DNA, antibodies, aptamers, and specific proteins. These sensing platforms have shown high efficacy for real-time detection of S. aureus using optical, electrochemical, and quartz crystal microbalance platforms [10,21,36,49]. In particular, antibody-based electrochemical biosensors for S. aureus detection have been shown to surpass aptamer-based biosensors in terms of bacterial capture efficiency [1,17]. However, the limited research on detecting S. aureus in real food samples and human blood serum hinders the practical applicability of these biosensors.

Combining nanomaterials and recognition elements in biosensors generates synergistic effects, amplifying signals from specific interactions with the target analyte, thereby significantly increasing sensitivity, selectivity, and overall performance [22]. Natural enzymes, known for their catalytic properties, are widely used as specialized reagents in the fabrication of electrochemical (bio)sensors, remarkably improving detection selectivity. However, their practical applications are constrained by issues such as susceptibility to denaturation under harsh conditions, rapid degradation, high costs, and difficulties in recycling. Therefore, there is a growing shift toward nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes, which mimic the functions of natural enzymes while overcoming these operational drawbacks [5]. These nanozymes present several advantages over natural enzymes, including increased stability, lower production costs, ease of manufacturing, and rapid catalytic activity [17,22]. Importantly, the development of electrochemical platforms utilizing the unique properties of nanoparticles with enzyme-like activity has provided significant benefits for analyte detection in terms of sensitivity and cost-effectiveness [11,55,57]. These nanozymes can act as alternatives to natural peroxidase-based systems, such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), which rely on labeling for target determination [11,18]. This capability allows for label-free detection, simplifying the detection process and improving efficiency. Carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are categorized as zero-dimensional nanomaterials with low toxicity and high biocompatibility, making them ideal candidates for nanozyme applications. Nonetheless, unlike CDs, GQDs have a graphene structure that provides better charge transfer, higher electron mobility, and a larger surface area, making them more effective for nanozyme applications [38,39,46].

In the past two decades, various biosensors have been developed using different methods to detect S. aureus, particularly in milk. Escamilla-Gómez et al. [16] identified S. aureus at 4.8 × 103 CFU mL−1, while Bhardwaj et al. [4] and Gao et al. [20] achieved detection at 10 CFU mL−1 using electrochemical sensors. Furthermore, Sahraneshin Samani et al. [37] developed an electrochemical sensor designed to detect micrococcal nuclease in milk. A common characteristic of these studies, which sets them apart from the current research, is that all spiked milk samples were diluted before contamination with different concentrations of S. aureus. In contrast, this study utilized GQDs, recognized for their excellent electron acceptor properties and advanced biomimetic capabilities, to detect S. aureus directly from milk samples without dilution, despite the complex matrix of milk. This represents a significant advancement over previous methods, which required dilution to achieve detection. Soares et al. [45] employed an artificial intelligence-based system known as the electronic tongue, which considered the protein composition and complex matrix structure of milk. They used impedance spectroscopy to show that S. aureus could theoretically be detected in milk at concentrations of 0.26 to 1.8 CFU mL−1. However, the present study provides the first experimental evidence confirming this approach.

Smartphone-based electrochemical systems use modern smartphone features, such as processing power and user-friendly interfaces, to perform electrochemical measurements and data analysis. These sensors typically consist of working (WE), reference (RE), and counter (CE) electrodes, integrated into a compact, portable device that connects to a smartphone via either a wired or a wireless connection [6,53]. By integrating the electrochemical advantages of smartphones, a variety of detectors can be developed for diverse applications. This enables the creation of field-deployable, user-friendly, and portable potentiostats that can be paired with immunosensor-based kits for rapid, on-site pathogen detection [2,53]. Such systems facilitate the identification, prevention, and monitoring of potential outbreaks. Moreover, the swift detection of pathogens in blood serum allows for targeted antibiotic treatment, minimizing the unnecessary use of broad-spectrum antibiotics [2].

This study introduces a novel approach for developing a smartphone-based electrochemical sensor tailored to the specific detection of S. aureus. Using the enzyme-mimicking properties of GQDs within a GQDs@monoclonal antibody system, the sensor enables direct detection of S. aureus in real samples such as milk and human serum. The process initiated with the formation of the GQDs@monoclonal antibody bio-complex, followed by its immobilization onto a gold electrode through chemical bonding (Scheme 1 ). The sensor's performance was experimentally evaluated in terms of sensitivity, specificity, and other parameters, accompanied by extensive characterizations to validate the successful bioconjugation. Real milk and human serum samples were then spiked with known concentrations of heat-inactivated S. aureus. The sensor's diagnostic potential was confirmed by significant selectivity and sensitivity with a very low limit of detection (LOD). This study is the first of its kind, designed for direct field use without pre-treatment. It would be promising for enabling early detection of S. aureus outbreaks, identifying contamination sources, monitoring the pathogen in food, and tracking and controlling mastitis in animals.

GQDs and heat-inactivated human serum were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Steinheim, Germany). The functional groups (e.g., carboxyl group) of GQDs were activated using 1-ethyl-3-(−3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, M.W.: 191.70 g mol−1, Novabiochem) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %, M.W.: 115.09 g mol– 1, Sigma Aldrich Co). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) tablets and sodium acetate buffer (NaAc) were also purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co. (Steinheim, Germany). Ethanolamine and bovine serum albumin (BSA) for blocking were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Steinheim, Germany). Double-distilled ultrapure water was sourced from a Millipore Direct-Q 3 UV (Millipore, Germany). An S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibody was provided by BacTrace, Sera Care (Krefeld, Germany). Heat-killed Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Enterococcus faecium, and Streptococcus pneumoniae were also supplied by BacTrace, Sera Care (Krefeld, Germany) as positive controls for cross-reactivity testing. Blood Agar, tryptic soy agar (TSA), Luria-Bertani (LB) broth, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 % concentration, M.W.: 36.46 g mol−1), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 35 %, 34.01 g mol−1), and absolute ethanol (99.9 %) were obtained from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). Screen-printed gold electrodes (SPGEs, DS220AT) were purchased from Metrohm-DropSens (Metrohm, Germany). Pasteurized milk was purchased from a local supermarket (Bandırma, Türkiye).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation and heat inactivation of S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus ATCC (25923) was cultured on TSA plates at 37 °C overnight to ensure high purity. After an 18-h incubation, a single colony was transferred to 5 mL of LB medium and incubated at 37 °C overnight. Bacterial suspensions at different concentrations were then prepared by diluting the culture in PBS (pH 7.2). To determine bacterial concentration, optical density (OD) values were converted to CFU mL−1 by plating different OD values of S. aureus and counting the resulting colonies [4]. A calibration curve of OD600 versus CFU mL−1 was generated for the strain. After the experiment, all bacterial samples were heat-inactivated by incubation in HEPES (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid) buffer, with the temperature gradually increased from 37 °C to 100 °C, over a 20-min period to preserve the antigenic structure of the bacteria. The inactivation was confirmed by inoculating the heat-killed S. aureus on blood agar and ensuring no bacterial growth [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of screen-printed working electrode with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: SPGEs consisted of a silver RE and gold-plated WE and CE on ceramic substrate. The WE had an average diameter of 35 μm and an approximate size of 4 mm. To remove organic residues, the SPGEs were initially washed with 70 % ethanol [39] followed by three thorough rinses with deionized water. The electrodes were then dried under a moderate flow of nitrogen gas. After drying, they were placed in perforated, sterile, plastic vacuum-sealed bags to prevent environmental exposure and minimize the risk of contamination.

Electrochemical (voltammetric and amperometric) measurements and characterization were performed using the Metrohm-DropSens electrochemical measurement system, coupled with the Metrohm-DropSens compact electrochemical interface. All analyses were conducted at room temperature (23 ± 1 °C). The electrochemical setup employed a three-electrode configuration, consisting of a gold working electrode modified with GQDs, an Ag/AgCl RE, and a gold CE. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and amperometric measurements were each repeated three times to ensure the reproducibility of sensor signals during the fabrication process. Voltammetric measurements were carried out within a current range of 100 nA–10 mA and a potential range of +1.5 V to −1.5 V, using a scan rate of −0.1 V to 1 V over three cycles. For amperometric measurements, the equilibration time (t-eq) was set to 0 s, the applied potential (Edc) to 0.9 V, the time interval between successive readings (t-int) to 0.1 s, and the total measurement duration to 120 s. Different electrochemical techniques were applied using separate SPGEs for each measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of GQD and antibody concentrations for S. aureus immunosensor

ce:section.ce:para: Six different concentrations of GQDs (0.005, 0.0125, 0.02, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1) were prepared in a two-layer system using distilled water and applied to the bare electrode surface to determine the optimal GQD concentration. The selection was based on the signal-to-concentration relationship for H₂O₂ reduction [29]. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate, with the optimal GQD concentration identified according to the average electrochemical sensor responses from the bioassays. After drop-coating the GQD layers onto the SPGE surface, the functional groups of the GQDs were activated via EDC-NHS coupling chemistry. A freshly prepared mixture of 0.4 M EDC and 0.1 M NHS (1:1 v/v) was incubated for 10 min. The GQD-laminated sensor surface was then thoroughly washed with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) before covalent immobilization of the S. aureus monoclonal antibody.

The antibody solution was prepared in 10 mM NaAc buffer (pH 4.5) at concentrations of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg mL−1 and incubated for 60 min to determine the optimal antibody concentration. After immobilization, the SPGE surface was rinsed with PBS buffer and double-distilled water to remove any unbound antibodies [39]. To minimize non-specific interactions during analyte detection, residual carboxyl groups on the surface were blocked by sequential incubation with 100 μg mL−1 BSA in PBS buffer and 1 M ethanolamine in double-distilled water (pH 8.5) for 4 min each. All antibody immobilization steps were performed at room temperature in a Petri dish to avoid contamination from dust and evaporation. Reagents (i.e., the antibody, BSA, and ethanolamine) were applied to the sensor surface in a fixed volume of 35 μL, and the surface was carefully rinsed with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) between each step to ensure optimal functionalization [39]. To determine the optimal antibody concentration, the sensor surface modified with different antibody concentrations was exposed to 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 antigen, and amperometric signal values were compared. The concentration producing the maximum response was selected as the optimal antibody concentration. For further validation, fluorescence spectrophotometric measurements were performed at 530 nm on both the native GQD and antibody-modified GQD. The changes in fluorescence intensity were analyzed to confirm successful bioconjugation between the GQD and antibody.

The surface topography of bare gold, GQD-laminated, and antibody-immobilized sensor surfaces was analyzed using atomic force microscopy (AFM; Naio, Nanosurf AG, Liestal, Switzerland). Measurements were performed with commercially available AFM probes (NCLR, NanoWorld AG, Liestal, Switzerland) with a spring constant of 23.191 N m−1, a resonant frequency of 170,481 Hz, a scan size of 3.027 μm, and a scan speed of 2 s. All AFM analyses were carried out in intermittent air mode at room temperature. To determine changes in surface topography, scans were conducted in contact mode on two areas (5 μm × 5 μm and 1 μm × 1 μm) as described by Savas et al. [41].

The samples were prepared by gently dipping a copper grid with a carbon lacey support (Plano GmbH) into a small amount of dried sample. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were captured using a JEOL JEM 2100 TEM (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Cary 630 FTIR, Agilent Technologies, Germany) equipped with a single reflection diamond attenuated total reflection (ATR) module was used to characterize chemo-functional groups in liquid samples of GQD, GQD-antibody, and GQD-antibody-antigen bioconjugations in the range of 4000–650 cm−1.

A DataPhysics optical contact angle meter was used to measure the contact angle of bare GQDs, antibody-immobilized GQDs, and GQD-antibody bioconjugates following antigen binding. A water droplet was placed on the surface of each GQD, and the measurements were taken at three different locations. The acquired datasets were analyzed using the SCA 20 software (version 5.0, DataPhysics, Filderstadt, Germany).

Commercially available GQDs typically exhibit an excitation wavelength of 485 nm and an emission wavelength of 530 nm [26]. Accordingly, the absorption of GQDs (500 μg mL−1) was measured before and after binding with a S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibody (25 μg mL−1), as well as following conjugation with the target antigen (106 CFU mL−1). Absorbance measurements were performed over the 200 to 800 nm range using a Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Fluorescence intensity was analyzed using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA).

The monitoring efficacy of the immunosensor was evaluated in three different matrices: PBS, human blood serum, and milk. Samples were spiked with S. aureus at different concentrations from 1 to 106 CFU mL−1. In PBS, assays were conducted over a 1–106 CFU mL−1 range using 25 μg mL−1 GQDs to establish baseline performance. In contrast, due to the complex and heterogeneous composition of human blood serum and milk, a higher GQD concentration (500 μg mL−1) was required to enhance detection sensitivity. A 200 μL aliquot of each prepared sample was applied to electrode surfaces functionalized with S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibodies, followed by a 5-min incubation at 0.9 V in 5 mM H₂O₂ for 110 s to facilitate amperometric measurements. The LOD was calculated using the formula 3.3 × (δ/m), where δ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements and m is the slope of the calibration curve (μA (CFU mL−1)−1), obtained from linear regression of the current response versus bacterial concentration. Blank measurements were performed in triplicate (n = 3) to determine δ, and calibration curves were constructed using at least five different bacterial concentrations for each matrix.

The immunosensor specificity was evaluated by coating the sensor surface with an S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibody and exposing it to L. monocytogenes, S. epidermidis, E. faecium, and St. pneumoniae at the same concentrations. Each pathogen was prepared at 106 CFU mL−1 in PBS. Samples were incubated at room temperature for 10 min in H₂O₂ redox indicator solution, exceeding the binding time required for S. aureus, before electrochemical amperometric measurements. Experiments were conducted in triplicate using a 35 μL application volume to confirm reproducibility. The percentage of cross-reactivity was calculated for each bacterium based on signal values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S. aureus concentration in culture media and post-heat inactivation
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus ATCC 25923 was cultured on TSA plates at 37 °C overnight to ensure high purity. After 18 h of incubation, a single colony was transferred into 5 mL of LB medium and incubated overnight at 37 °C. The next day, 2 mL of the bacterial culture was transferred into a spectrophotometer cuvette, and the OD₆₀₀ value was measured as 1.638. For serial dilution, eight serial dilution tubes were prepared, each containing 900 μL of LB medium. A 100 μL aliquot was transferred from the first tube (OD₆₀₀ = 1.638) into the second tube, and the OD₆₀₀ was measured again, yielding a value of 1.410. This dilution process was sequentially repeated for all eight tubes, resulting in OD₆₀₀ values of 1.194, 0.920, 0.645, 0.574, 0.317, 0.2, and 0.1 for the third to eighth tubes, respectively. A 20 μL sample from each dilution tube was plated onto TSA plates, followed by a 12-h incubation. The eighth plate contained 13 colonies, while the seventh plate had 124 pure colonies. TEM images of the samples are shown in Fig. 1A, with a summary of the experimental procedures presented in Fig. 1B. Using a 10-fold dilution series and the OD₆₀₀ values, the bacterial concentration corresponding to an OD₆₀₀ of 1.638 was calculated to be 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1. A calibration curve (Fig. 1C) was constructed, and regression analysis of the standard curve within the concentration range of 13 to 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 resulted in an R2 value of 0.99, confirming a strong correlation between OD₆₀₀ values and bacterial concentration.

A variety of characterization techniques were employed to investigate the morphology, elemental composition, particle orientation, and topography of the selected commercial GQD nanomaterial. HR-TEM was used to determine the homogeneous size and morphology of the GQDs. TEM images revealed that the GQDs had a size range of approximately 8 to 10 nm (Fig. 2A). AFM is a powerful tool to visualize and quantify surface changes at different stages of biosensor preparation. AFM images shown in Fig. 2B and C illustrate the surface topography of GQDs on the SPGE, with a height of 2 nm at the 1 × 1 μm scale and 21 nm at the 3 × 3 μm scale. Fig. 2D displays fluorescence and UV–vis spectra, indicating that the GQDs exhibited fluorescence with an emission wavelength of 530 nm and an excitation wavelength of 485 nm. The FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of various chemo-functional groups on the GQD surfaces. As shown in Fig. 2E, the broad peak around 3300–3400 cm−1 corresponds to hydroxyl (OH) stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. The peak at 1720 cm−1, attributed to CO stretching vibration, suggests the presence of carboxylic acid or carbonyl groups at the edges. A peak near 1620 cm−1, associated with aromatic CC bonds within the sp2 carbon network, reinforces the structural integrity and stability of the underlying graphene framework [12]. The CV measurement over a voltage range from −1.5 V to +1.5 V [3] was utilized to characterize the electrochemical properties of the GQDs. The optimal voltage for amperometric measurements was determined by identifying the maximum current peaks corresponding to the redox reaction. The optimum potential for amperometric measurements was determined to be +0.9 V, where the redox reaction showed maximum activity (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of smartphone-compatible electrochemical signal measurement device
Hardware for signal detection in electrochemical biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: A potentiostat controls the biosensor (Fig. 3A), performing amperometric and CV measurements. This device integrates the high-precision AD5941 impedance and electrochemical front-end circuit (Analog Devices, US) for accurate voltage control and current measurement. The circuit sweeps DC voltages from -2 V to +2 V between the RE and WE, and measures currents with a resolution of 1.5 nA over the WE. It also includes a potentiostat buffer between the RE and CE, along with electrode-connection buffers. The analog front end includes a 12-bit digital-to-analog converter (DAC), a trans-impedance amplifier (TIA) with 26 selectable gains (200 Ω to 96 kΩ), and a programmable gain amplifier with a maximum gain of 9. An ARM Cortex-M4-based STM32L433 microcontroller (STMicroelectronics, US) with an 80 MHz core clock, USB, and SPI interfaces controls the front end. The microcontroller communicates with the front end via SPI. Most input signal processing occurs within the front end, which integrates a 16-bit, 1.6 MSPS analog-to-digital converter (ADC) and multiple analog filters for noise suppression and anti-aliasing. Digital filters, including finite impulse response (FIR) and notch filters, enhance noise reduction and provide 50/60 Hz mains rejection. These signal processing features enable current measurement with a resolution down to 1 nA at a 1000 SPS data acquisition rate. To fully utilize the AD5941 and manage its registers via SPI, command libraries facilitate voltage and gain control. The software, developed in C using STM32CubeIDE (STMicroelectronics, US), includes electrochemical experiment protocols, an automatic gain determination algorithm, and noise-reducing filter controls. The device is powered and connected via USB when linked to a mobile system for operation. The circuit was designed with Autodesk Eagle EDA (Autodesk, US) electronic design automation software. The component layout is optimized to fit within a 15 × 22 mm2 quad-layer printed circuit board (PCB), with components mounted on both sides (Fig. 3B). The circuit board is enclosed in a sleek aluminum casing, which is connected to the ground (GND) pin for shielding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Software for controlling the developed hardware
ce:section.ce:para: A mobile application for the Android operating system was developed to conduct electrochemical measurements using the potentiostat system (Fig. 3C). The application features three main functional modules: (1) an Experiment Setup screen for inputting experiment parameters; (2) an Experiments List screen for viewing conducted experiments; and (3) a Settings screen for managing in-app configurations and session control. Users can enter experiment parameters and initiate the experiment by pressing the Start button. The parameters are transmitted via USB to the microcontroller, which executes the experiment based on the selected settings. During the experiment, the microcontroller continuously streams measured current and voltage values for each sampled signal point, along with elapsed time via USB communication. The mobile application then plots the data in real time. After the experiment ends, all data related to it is saved to a file on the mobile device (Fig. 3D).

Due to variations in gain resistor values, Op-Amp offsets and drifts, and reference voltage levels often exceeding measurement noise by an order of magnitude, calibration was essential for achieving accurate readings in the developed electronic system. To correct these deviations, ADC, DAC, voltage references, and TIA gains of AD5941 were measured with reference devices. The measured deviations were subsequently stored as correction constants into first-degree equations embedded within the microcontroller's code.

Amperometric electrochemical measurements were conducted on SPGEs at 0.9 V for 120 s using GQD concentrations at 1, 0.5, 0.1, 0.02, 0.0125, and 0.005 mg mL−1 concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4(A–C), applying 35 μL of PBS buffer to an SPGE coated with 1 mg mL−1 GQDs resulted in an average current response of 35 μA. When the same GQD concentration was tested with 35 μL of milk, the amperometric response decreased to 4 μA, while in blood serum, it was further reduced to 0.5 μA. Based on these findings, the optimal GQD concentrations for obtaining the lowest LOD were determined to be 0.0125 mg mL−1 in PBS buffer, 0.5 mg mL−1 in milk, and 1 mg mL−1 in blood serum. In milk, the signal response showed no significant difference between 0.5 and 1 mg mL−1 GQDs; therefore, 0.5 mg mL−1 was chosen as the optimal level, as the marginal signal increase at 1 mg mL−1 did not significantly improve the LOD. In blood serum, the signal from 1 mg mL−1 GQDs was reduced eightfold compared to milk, decreasing from 4.1 μA to 0.53 μA. Hence, 1 mg mL−1 was determined as the optimal concentration for bioassays involving blood samples. Since PBS lacks complex components that could attenuate the GQD signal, the response from 1 mg mL−1 GQDs in milk (3.9 μA) was comparable to that from 0.0125 mg mL−1 GQDs in PBS (3.9 μA). Fig. 4D shows a comparative summary of the measurement values for all three samples.

In both milk and blood serum, the complex biological matrix is a key factor suppressing the GQD activity during electrochemical measurements. In milk, high protein (e.g., casein and whey) and lipid content can adsorb onto the electrode surface, causing fouling. This fouling obstructs the active surface areas of GQDs, preventing electron transfer and reducing both catalytic activity and signal intensity [30,43]. In blood serum, high ionic strength, pH variations, parasitic redox-active substances, and abundant albumin, along with other organic/inorganic components and ions (especially Na+, K+, and Ca2+ ions), adsorb onto the GQD surface. This leads to the coverage of active sites, hindering electron transfer and reducing the GQD effect in serum measurements. Consequently, the high electrochemical signal observed at higher GQD concentrations is significantly diminished compared to environments free of such complex components, such as PBS buffer [32,51].

Before detecting S. aureus in various environments, the optimal concentration of S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibody was investigated (Figs. 4E-4G). Four concentrations (10, 25, 40, and 50 μg mL−1) were immobilized separately onto the GQD-coated SPGE surfaces. After immobilization, S. aureus (124 × 109 CFU mL−1) was introduced onto each antibody-modified SPGE, and real-time amperometric measurements were recorded after a 20-min incubation [8].

Compared to the bare GQD signal, the amperometric responses were suppressed by 8 % with 10 μg mL−1 antibody, 91 % with 25 μg mL−1 antibody, 90 % with 40 μg mL−1 antibody, and 91 % with 50 μg mL−1 antibody (Fig. 4E). Similarly, fluorescence spectrophotometry revealed fluorescence quenching of 6 %, 94 %, and 92 % at 25, 40, and 50 μg mL−1, respectively, compared to bare GQD. Both methods confirmed that 10 μg mL−1 was insufficient for S. aureus detection (Fig. 4F). The electrochemical signal responses and fluorescence intensities for 25, 40, and 50 μg mL−1 antibody concentrations showed negligible differences. Therefore, 25 μg mL−1 was selected as the optimal antibody concentration to minimize costs in developing the GQD-based electrochemical immunosensor (Fig. 4G). Similar antibody concentrations have been reported in other studies, despite differences in the target analytes. For example, Mansuriya & Altintas [29] found 25 μg mL−1 to be optimal for their nanocomposite-based immunosensor for cardiac biomarker detection, while Wenninger et al. [50] employed 20 μg mL−1 in a paper-based electrochemical immunosensor for symmetric dimethylarginine detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S. aureus detection in PBS, human blood serum, and milk
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of S. aureus was performed using amperometry within a concentration range of 1.24–1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 in PBS buffer, milk, and blood serum samples. In our previous studies, we optimized the detection time for antigen binding in milk and blood serum, establishing a direct incubation period of 5–7 min applied to the system [38,40,41]. In this study, the universal threshold value of 2 CFU mL−1 for S. aureus, which is regarded as the minimum level at which the bacterium may cause infection in humans and milk (Regulation 2073/2005 - EN - EUR-Lex, n.d.) was considered. Exceeding this threshold increases the risk of infection and the onset of disease symptoms, depending on an individual's immune system.

The results of S. aureus detection by amperometry in PBS, milk, and blood serum are presented in Fig. 5 . It was observed that as the S. aureus concentration increased, the electrochemical signal values decreased (Figs. 5A-D). In each medium, the maximum signal obtained at the highest GQD concentration and the minimum detectable S. aureus concentration are shown in Fig. 5, which also illustrates the relative signal reduction for S. aureus concentrations within the 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1 range, confirming that the signal suppression was directly proportional to bacterial concentration. All reported LOD values are theoretical, derived from statistical parameters of the calibration curves rather than from visual estimation or practical detection thresholds. Based on the LOD calculation described in Section 2.7, a linear relationship was observed in PBS buffer over the concentration range of 2 to 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1, with an R2 value of 0.99 and an LOD of 1 CFU mL−1. In milk and blood serum samples, the corresponding linear ranges were 12.4 to 1.24 × 109 and 124 to 1.24 × 109 CFU mL−1, with R2 values of 0.98 and 0.99, and LODs of 4 CFU mL−1 and 34 CFU mL−1, respectively.

One major challenge in detecting S. aureus from blood and milk samples is the dilution step before spiking with a known concentration of S. aureus. This dilution reduces the concentration of proteins and ions in the matrix, which may otherwise block the sensor surface. Studies using spike immunosensors, where S. aureus is added at various concentrations after dilution, have reported LODs ranging from 10 to 100 CFU mL−1 [4,16]. Roushani et al. [36] detected S. aureus at 100 CFU mL−1 after pre-treating milk and blood serum. In another study, Han et al. [21] developed an S. aureus-specific immunosensor using AuPdPt trimetallic nanoparticles, achieving an LOD of 39 CFU mL−1 in PBS buffer, while detection in pure milk yielded an LOD of 3.41 × 103 CFU mL−1. Evdokimov et al. [17] also developed an immunosensor based on a polyelectrolyte-modified screen-printed carbon electrode, using CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques to detect S. aureus at concentrations of 230 and 1000 CFU mL−1, respectively. Plate counting provides a comparative analysis of electrochemical sensor studies developed for detecting S. aureus in milk, blood, and other samples.

To assess the sensor's performance in real-world applications, the bacterial concentration in identical samples of milk and blood serum was quantified using both the conventional colony count method and the GQD-based immunosensor. The average concentrations of S. aureus detected in PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum were 2, 124, and 240 CFU mL−1, respectively, as measured by both techniques. Correspondingly, the GQD-based immunosensor yielded consistent results, demonstrating its reliability in complex matrices. The spiked recovery rates were calculated to be 100 %, 108 %, and 103 % for PBS, undiluted milk, and blood serum, respectively, further validating the sensor's accuracy and precision. These findings confirmed that the GQD-based immunosensor is highly effective for detecting S. aureus in real samples, making it a promising tool for applications in food safety and clinical diagnostics. Conventional colony counting requires a lengthy processing time of at least 30 h and considerable operator involvement. In contrast, the sensors developed in this study required no pre-treatment, present rapid results, and are sufficiently user-friendly for widespread application. Therefore, these sensors have the potential for extension to other real sample analyses.

To minimize the risk of contamination, heat-inactivated S. aureus was used in all detection experiments. As a Gram-positive bacterium, S. aureus features a thick and resilient peptidoglycan layer, which provides inherent resistance to heat. Importantly, surface antigens such as Protein A are known to be heat-stable. To preserve the structural integrity of these antigens, S. aureus cells were heat-inactivated under controlled conditions in HEPES buffer. The temperature was gradually increased from 37 °C to 100 °C, and the cells were maintained at 100 °C for the remainder of the 20-min treatment. HEPES was selected for its pH-buffering capacity during heating, which helped maintain protein structure. This protocol provided complete bacterial inactivation while preserving surface antigen integrity.

Literature reports support the claim that such mild thermal inactivation can effectively eliminate bacterial viability without compromising the immunological integrity of key surface antigens. For instance, Kennedy et al. [24] demonstrated that heat treatments within the 50–80 °C range induced only minimal changes in the antibody-binding capacity of S. aureus, preserving antigenic structures critical for serological assays. Similarly, a comprehensive review by Bröker et al. [9] and other supporting studies [27,31,33,34] confirmed that heat-inactivated S. aureus retained the ability to elicit antibody-mediated immune responses in both humans and animals. Therefore, the antigenic profile of S. aureus subjected to controlled thermal inactivation closely resembles that of live bacteria. No significant differences in antibody-binding efficiency have been reported, supporting the relevance and applicability of the findings to real-world detection scenarios involving live S. aureus cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S. aureus binding with GQD-antibody bioconjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The successful binding of S. aureus and the GQD-antibody bioconjugate was confirmed using fluorescence spectroscopy, contact angle measurements, AFM, and HR-TEM. The 3D surface topology images of the bare GQD, with scan areas of 3 × 3 μm2 and 1 × 1 μm2, displayed heights of 21 nm and 2 nm, respectively (Fig. 2B and C). After the GQD-coated sensor surface was immobilized with antibody, the surface was treated with antigen, and 3D surface topology images were obtained after the formation of the antigen-antibody complex. Heights of 35 nm (Fig. 6A) and 18 nm (Fig. 6B) were recorded in 3 × 3 μm2 and 1 × 1 μm2 scan areas, respectively. The observed increase in surface height in both areas confirmed the successful preparation of the sensing surface for bacterial detection experiments.

Contact angle measurements provided valuable characterization data on the changes in surface wettability during the modification steps of GQD, GQD-antibody complex, and GQD-antibody-antigen complex (Figs. 6C-5E). The bare GQDs showed a contact angle of approximately 30° (Fig. 6C), consistent with values reported in the literature for GQDs [14]. This is attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQD surface, such as OH, carboxyl (-COOH), and epoxy groups, which form strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules, resulting in a relatively small contact angle. For the GQD-antibody complex, the contact angle increased to around 65° (Fig. 6D). This increase is due to the covalent immobilization of antibodies onto the GQDs via EDC/NHS coupling through carboxyl groups, which alters the surface properties. The introduction of hydrophobic protein structures contributes to a higher contact angle (Fig. 6D). After antigen binding to form the antigen–GQD–antibody complex, the surface's hydrophilic properties increase again because of protein-protein interactions and the exposure of hydrophilic residues. The presence of charged amino acid residues in the antigen increases the surface's affinity for water, decreasing the contact angle to approximately 52° (Fig. 6E).

An HR-TEM image with a 500 nm scale bar (Fig. 6F) shows S. aureus bacteria conjugated with antibodies and GQDs. The spherical S. aureus cells, ranging from 300 to 600 nm in diameter, are clearly visible. Aggregated antibodies, which are capturing the bacteria, and almost spherical GQDs on their surfaces can also be observed (Fig. 6F). In Fig. 6G, 10 nm GQDs on the antibody surfaces are more distinctly visualized as high-contrast dots under a 100 nm scale, demonstrating their precise localization [7,23,25].

The interaction between the sensor immobilized with S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibodies and non-specific bacteria was investigated to determine the specificity of the immunosensor developed for S. aureus [42]. For this purpose, heat-killed reference samples of Gram-positive bacteria L. monocytogenes and S. epidermidis, which are commonly found in milk alongside S. aureus [54], as well as E. faecium and St. pneumoniae, which cause symptoms similar to S. aureus infections in humans [48], were separately prepared in milk and human serum at a fixed concentration of 106 CFU mL−1. Each sample was injected onto antibody-immobilized surfaces and measured over 110 s. The bacterial concentration chosen for cross-reactivity testing was intentionally high. Although minimal cross-reactivity was recorded for all reference pathogens in milk, the relative cross-reactivity of non-specific pathogenic bacteria was higher in human serum compared to milk (Table 1 ). However, the maximum cross-reaction observed with another Staphylococcus species remained at an acceptable level (12.4 %). These results demonstrate the exceptional specificity of the biosensor for the target bacterium.

This study successfully developed a portable, smartphone-compatible GQD-based electrochemical immunosensor for the direct detection of S. aureus in complex biological and food matrices. The immunosensor was optimized by assessing the GQD concentration, antibody immobilization efficiency, and detection conditions in PBS, milk, and human blood serum. High accuracy was achieved with the system's circuit board, integrated with CV and amperometric analyses. Characterization through fluorescence spectroscopy, AFM, HR-TEM, and contact angle measurements confirmed successful conjugation of GQDs with S. aureus-specific monoclonal antibodies. The electrochemical performance of the sensor demonstrated a low LOD of 4 CFU mL−1, meeting relevant food safety and clinical diagnostic standards. The designed biosensing platform eliminates the need for sample dilution or extensive pre-treatment, which is a common limitation in traditional immunosensors. The sensor revealed high sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility, making it a promising tool for rapid, on-site pathogen detection. Furthermore, it showed strong correlation with conventional plate counting methods, while providing significantly faster results. Future work should focus on improving the sensor's stability and robustness for prolonged use in industrial and healthcare settings. Further surface modifications could enhance resistance to matrix interference, ensuring higher sensitivity in complex sample environments. The development of multiplexed detection systems could expand its applicability to simultaneous identification of multiple pathogens, thereby enhancing its diagnostic potential. Large-scale field validation studies in food processing facilities and clinical environments are necessary to assess performance under real-world conditions and support commercial translation of this technology.
